text: This is the view through a rigid oblique viewing thoracoscope inserted into the left pleural cavity.
The front of the patient is to the picture's left and the back to the picture's right.
The left side of the patient is beyond the top of the picture and the right side towards the picture's bottom.
The headward direction is into the picture while the direction of the patient's feet is behind us.
The pinkish organ in front of us with the dark markings is the patient's left lung.
This line is the oblique fissure and it divides the lung into an upper lobe and a lower lobe.
A pair of curved sponge holding forceps is used to bring the apex of the left upper lobe into view.
Seen here is the posterior mediastinal tumor in the back of the chest wall, which will be resected later.
Here is the bulla in the apex of the left upper lobe.
together with an area of associated scarring which will be removed as part of this operation.
Another pair of sponge holding forceps is used to grasp the bullous apex of the left lung.
The area of scarring seen here is the site of previous bullae which have ruptured and healed.
An endoscopic stapler and cutter is introduced into the left pleural space and its jaws are then opened to encompass the base of the bulla between them.
This includes some of the area of apical scarring as seen here.
Then the device is definitively closed and fired.
As the staples only reach this line at the end, when the stapler is opened, we will see that the bridge of tissue still connects the bullous part of the lung to the normal part of the lung.
A second firing of the stapler completes the biopsy and the specimen can now be retrieved.
The sample is then sent to the laboratories for histopathological examination.
The staple line is then checked for bleeding or lung tissue tears.
This is the mass to be resected.
It is ovoid, well circumscribed, and has the splanctic nerve overlying it.
This is the distal arch and descending portion of the thoracic aorta.
Behind the mass to be resected are the first rib, the second rib, the third rib, and the fourth rib.
Between the aorta and the mass, a clear plane is seen.
This corresponds with the preoperative CT scan, which shows that the mass does not compress or invade adjacent structures.
The posterior minithoracotomy, to the right-hand side of our view, has been extended and an Alexis soft tissue retractor is inserted into the wound.
The upper lobe of the left lung is retracted out of the way.
Then the pleura overlying the mass is incised from below.
The plane around the splanchnic nerve is soft, however the mass is densely adherent to the nerve, and so a section of nerve will be resected on block together with the mass.
Behind the splanchnic nerve, a clear plane is found under the mass.
The plane in front of the mass between it and the aorta is opened.
The splanchnic nerve below the mass is divided.
The surrounding plane of tissue underneath the mass is now clearly visible and is developed with a combination of hooked diathermy
and blunt dissection.
To gain a view of the splanchnic nerve above the mass, the endoscope is rotated, allowing us to look under and past the mass.
One of the small blood vessels accompanying the nerve is treated with diathermy to control bleeding.
This is the splanchnic nerve above the mass.
The endoscope has been rotated.
The patient's head is beyond the left of the picture, their feet behind us and to the right, their right side towards the bottom of the picture and the left side towards the top.
The splanchnic nerve above the mass is divided using hooked diathermy.
The mass is then retrieved and sent to the laboratories for histopathological examination.
The tumor bed is examined once more.
In addition to bleeding points and control of hemostasis, any residual tumor left behind is also sought.
Bleeding points are treated with diathermy.
Occasionally, bleeding points or lymphatic leaks may require over-sewing.
A small volume of diluted hydrogen peroxide solution is instilled into the chest cavity as a hemostatic adjunct.
Then the chest cavity is washed out.
A raw area found on the surface of the upper lobe of the left lung is reinforced with fine prolimonofilament suture.
A small sheet of polyglactin mesh is placed over the tumour bed and aerosolised fibrin sealant is sprayed over it.
The raw area oversown earlier is also overlaid with the mesh and sealant.
A chest drain is then inserted and its tip placed in the highest point of the pleural space, the cupola.
The lung is re-expanded under endoscopic vision to confirm good expansion throughout.
The alexis retractor is removed, hemostasis is secured, then the endoscope is withdrawn and the wounds are closed, concluding the operation.
